CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (weakly supervised PSL models leveraging lexical, behavioral and social features; dataset assembly) are clearly stated early. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is thorough, making the narrative easy to follow. However presentation omissions (no reported hyperparameters, limited detail on notation consistency and on the effectiveness of figures/tables in the provided summary) leave some parts harder to assess and suggest minor clarity gaps that should be filled for full reproducibility. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (weakly supervised PSL models, incorporation of behavioral/social predicates, and the Congressional tweets dataset) are presented early and clearly. Notation and methodological descriptions are generally consistent — predicates like SAMETIME, RETWEETS, FOLLOWS and the FRAME(T,F) target are understandable — though a few modeling/hyperparameter details (e.g., no hyperparameter reporting) are missing. Figures/tables and concrete numbers (184,914 tweets; 21.52 point F1 improvement; 73.4% inter-annotator agreement) appear to be used effectively to support claims. Writing quality is good with only minor jargon density around PSL/HL-MRF terminology that a non-specialist reader might need to parse.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (weakly supervised PSL models leveraging lexical, behavioral and social features; dataset assembly) are clearly stated early. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is thorough, making the narrative easy to follow. However presentation omissions (no reported hyperparameters, limited detail on notation consistency and on the effectiveness of figures/tables in the provided summary) leave some parts harder to assess and suggest minor clarity gaps that should be filled for full reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (weakly supervised PSL models, incorporation of behavioral/social predicates, and the Congressional tweets dataset) are presented early and clearly. Notation and methodological descriptions are generally consistent — predicates like SAMETIME, RETWEETS, FOLLOWS and the FRAME(T,F) target are understandable — though a few modeling/hyperparameter details (e.g., no hyperparameter reporting) are missing. Figures/tables and concrete numbers (184,914 tweets; 21.52 point F1 improvement; 73.4% inter-annotator agreement) appear to be used effectively to support claims. Writing quality is good with only minor jargon density around PSL/HL-MRF terminology that a non-specialist reader might need to parse.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the experimental comparison is fair in that it uses the same dataset and metric, but narrow and incomplete because it omits stronger supervised, neural, and contemporary weak/distant-supervision baselines as well as ablation analyses. Given these consistent concerns, a mid-level score reflects that the comparisons are legitimate but insufficient to support the broad empirical claims without additional baselines or ablation studies.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its PSL models only to a lexical unigram baseline, so key and stronger supervised or recent neural baselines (and a supervised oracle) are missing—this is especially problematic given the annotated subset and the strong empirical claim of +21.52 F1. Comparisons appear to use the same data/metric, but there is no indication of ablation isolating behavioral/social predicates or of fair supervised baselines; the authors do discuss dataset/codebook mismatches but do not appear to acknowledge or compare against competing methods that could perform comparably. Overall the empirical comparison is inadequate for the claim of substantive improvement.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its PSL weakly-supervised models against a lexical unigram baseline and demonstrates large gains (21.52 unsupervised F1 points), using the same Twitter dataset and evaluation metric, which is appropriate but narrow. From my applied-NLP perspective this is useful but incomplete: it omits comparison to other contemporary weakly-/distant-supervision methods, supervised classifiers trained on the annotated subset, or neural text models that are relevant baselines. The authors do provide thorough related-work discussion, but the empirical comparisons do not fully analyze when prior methods match or beat their approach nor include stronger baselines, so the comparison is fair but not comprehensive.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a substantive contribution—large dataset and clear gains from leveraging behavioral/social signals—but is held back by missing analyses and reproducibility details (ablation/sensitivity studies, hyperparameters, code) and limited clarity on labeled data and baselines. Given the promising results and acknowledged limitations, but significant methodological gaps that prevent fully assessing robustness, a middle score of 3 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: From a methodological rigor standpoint the experiments are shallow. The paper reports large unsupervised gains over a unigram lexical baseline but lacks a strong supervised oracle baseline and gives no indication of the size of the annotated training set (annotated subset from 40 politicians suggests limited labeled data, <200 not excluded), which prevents assessing practical upper bounds or statistical significance. Ablation/sensitivity analyses are not clearly reported despite multiple behavioral/social predicates and model components, hyperparameters and code are not mentioned, and reproducibility details are insufficient. The paper does acknowledge some limitations (inter-annotator agreement, frame mismatch), but this does not compensate for the missing controlled analyses and baselines I require.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a substantial dataset (184,914 tweets) and a concrete weakly supervised PSL approach with a large reported gain (21.52 unsupervised F1 points) over a lexical baseline, which supports the central claim that behavioral/social signals help. However, the summary does not report ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., contribution of individual network predicates or hyperparameter sweeps) or hyperparameter/code release, limiting reproducibility. Related work coverage is said to be thorough and the authors acknowledge clear limitations (73.4% IAA, adaptation of frames), which I view positively. Overall the experiments are adequate to demonstrate promise but miss some important analyses and reproducibility details, so I rate it a 3.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper does not introduce a fundamentally new learning paradigm but contributes a meaningful and useful combination of behavioral/social predicates with lexical signals and a new annotated Congressional tweet dataset. Given the clear empirical gains and the non‑trivial integration of PSL/hinge‑loss MRFs for collective framing inference—even if largely engineering and application‑focused—this merits a moderate‑to‑strong originality score rather than a low one.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies established tools (PSL / hinge-loss MRFs, word2vec similarity, lexical baselines) to political framing but contributes a novel combination of behavioral/social predicates (SAMETIME, RETWEETS, FOLLOWS) and a new annotated Congressional tweet dataset—so the perspective and application are meaningful. However, the core modeling machinery and weak-supervision paradigm are not new and the work appears to be an engineering-driven extension rather than a fundamentally new method with formal guarantees. The authors do clearly differentiate their contributions from prior framing work and offer non-trivial, domain-specific feature engineering, which would be useful to practitioners but is best characterized as moderate novelty rather than a major methodological advance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a notable and practically useful novelty by applying weakly supervised PSL-based collective classification to political framing on Twitter and explicitly integrating behavioral/social predicates (SAMETIME, RETWEETS, FOLLOWS) with lexical signals—this combination is not a routine reuse of prior single-feature approaches. While PSL and weak supervision themselves are established, compiling lexical, temporal/retweet/follow signals into hinge‑loss MRF rules for frame inference and demonstrating large unsupervised F1 gains (21.52 points over a lexical baseline) constitutes a non‑trivial methodological extension. The authors also clearly separate their contribution from prior lexical baselines and bring a new annotated Twitter dataset of U.S. Congress tweets, which would inform experts though it stops short of introducing an entirely new learning paradigm.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a promising applied contribution and reports large empirical gains, but its evaluation is technically limited: comparisons are restricted to a lexical baseline, held-out labeled evaluation and statistical testing/hyperparameter details are missing, and some modeling assumptions and rule contributions are insufficiently ablated. Given the value of the dataset and the encouraging results, but weighed against these methodological gaps that preclude strong validity claims, I assign a middling score reflecting promise but insufficient technical evidence.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper makes clear claims and provides experimental results (e.g., a reported +21.52 unsupervised F1 over a lexical baseline), but the evaluation is technically weak: only a lexical baseline is used and no strong supervised oracle or held-out labeled evaluation (annotated sample appears to come from only 40 politicians, well below my 200-sample expectation), and hyperparameters/statistical tests are not reported. Mathematical content is limited to applying PSL without accompanying formal guarantees or derivations of identifiability/consistency for the weakly supervised setup. There are unsupported assumptions linking behavioral/social predicates to frame labels and insufficient ablation to isolate each rule’s contribution, so the conclusions are not fully justified by the presented evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents clear claims and provides empirical evidence (a reported +21.52 unsupervised F1 improvement using PSL models over a lexical unigram baseline) and introduces a sizeable Twitter dataset, which aligns with my applied-NLP priorities. However, the experimental methodology appears limited: comparisons rely on a single lexical baseline, hyperparameters and statistical significance testing are not reported, and code availability is unclear, making reproducibility and the robustness of the claimed gains uncertain. There are also potential unsupported assumptions about generality given the moderates inter-annotator agreement (73.4%) and the Twitter-specific modifications to the frame schema. Overall, the contribution is promising but some technical evidence and controls are missing to fully validate the claims.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important applied problem and demonstrates substantial empirical gains by incorporating behavioral and social signals, and that releasing the large tweet collection and model artifacts would be valuable to the community. However, Reviewer A’s concerns about limited annotated evaluation, lack of strong supervised baselines, and absence of theoretical analysis temper claims of broad impact. Given the promising practical improvements and useful data release but constrained validation and scope, a middle score reflects likely usefulness while recognizing limits on generality and theoretical contribution.

reviewer_a.justification: While the paper tackles an important applied problem—political framing on Twitter—and shows promising gains by integrating behavioral/social signals into a PSL weakly supervised model, its potential impact is limited by weak empirical validation and lack of theoretical guarantees. The annotated evaluation is small (only 40 politicians, <200 annotated samples implied) and no strong supervised oracle baseline or formal analysis (e.g., identifiability/consistency of the PSL rules) is provided, so it is unclear whether the reported 21.5-point F1 improvement generalizes beyond the lexical baseline. The approach could inspire follow-up work combining network features with weak supervision, but in its current form it is a modest, niche contribution rather than a broadly influential advance.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—automated issue framing on Twitter—using a label-efficient weakly supervised PSL approach that leverages behavioral and social signals; the reported 21.52-point average F1 gain over a lexical baseline is a substantial, practically meaningful improvement. The collection of 184,914 congressional tweets and planned release of the dataset and model rules materially increases the community’s ability to study political discourse with limited annotation. While scope is somewhat constrained to congressional tweets and inter-annotator agreement (73.4%) and code release are unclear, the method’s demonstrated benefit and data artifacts make this a strong, broadly useful contribution that will likely influence subsequent applied work in social-media NLP and weak supervision.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a relevant applied NLP/ML problem and contributes valuable resources and a collective-classification approach integrating lexical, behavioral, and social signals. Concerns remain about the small annotated subset, limited supervised baselines, and sparse reproducibility details, but the empirical gains and dataset scale justify acceptance for an applied top venue pending stronger evaluation and clearer reproducibility information.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—weakly supervised collective classification of political framing on Twitter—is within NLP/ML conference scope, and the use of PSL and social/behavioral features is relevant to the NLP community. However, from my Theory Purist perspective the empirical evaluation raises concerns for a top venue: the annotated subset is small (40 politicians, <200 annotated samples), no strong supervised oracle baseline is reported, and evaluation relies on unsupervised F1 versus a unigram lexical baseline, making it hard to judge substantive gains. The paper also lacks reported hyperparameters/code and offers limited theoretical guarantees about the proposed probabilistic model, which weakens its fit for top ML venues; overall this is a borderline fit (more suitable for applied NLP venues unless strengthened).

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely in the applied NLP space favored by top conferences: it develops weakly supervised PSL models that integrate lexical, behavioral, and social signals and reports a substantial empirical gain (≈21.5 F1 points) over a lexical baseline. The authors assemble and plan to release a large Twitter dataset (184,914 congressional tweets with a 40-politician annotated subset) and provide collective-classification rules, which has high practical value for the community. My only minor concern is practical reproducibility detail (no mention of hyperparameters or code release in the summary), but overall the work targets the right audience and uses appropriate evaluation for a top NLP/ML venue.

main_claims: Introduces weakly supervised probabilistic soft logic (PSL) models that leverage lexical, behavioral, and social information to predict issue frames in political tweets.

Combining lexical features of tweets with network-based behavioral features (temporal activity, retweets, follows) substantially improves performance—reporting an increase of 21.52 average unsupervised F1 points over a lexical baseline.

Assembles and annotates a dataset of U.S. Congress members' tweets (184,914 tweets collected; annotated subset from 40 politicians) and will release the tweets dataset and model rules to the community.
_summary.methodology: Designs a collection of weakly supervised collective classification models compiled into a hinge-loss Markov random field using PSL; inputs include adapted frame unigrams, MAXSIM word2vec similarity, party bigrams/trigrams, and behavioral/social predicates (SAMETIME, RETWEETS, FOLLOWS) to infer FRAME(T,F).
datasets: 184,914 tweets collected from U.S. Congress members (with an annotated subset from 40 politicians)
limitations: Inter-annotator agreement was 73.4%, indicating difficulty of frame classification for tweets.
Boydstun’s original Frame 15 (Other) was never used and was replaced with three Twitter-specific frames, indicating possible mismatch between the original codebook and Twitter data.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - political framing and collective classification on Twitter

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in statistical machine learning and formal methods with a focus on probabilistic graphical models and guarantees for learning algorithms.
traits: Penalizes papers that do not provide formal guarantees or theoretical analysis of proposed models (e.g., consistency, identifiability, or bounds).
Requires at least one strong supervised oracle baseline and expects comparable evaluation on held-out labeled data (penalizes if annotated sample < 200 for claims of performance).
Discounts empirical gains if they hinge primarily on engineering of features or ad-hoc rules rather than principled modeling innovations.
Penalizes papers that introduce heuristics without ablation isolating each component's contribution (expects ablation for each rule/predicate).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I evaluate primarily on theoretical novelty and rigor; empirical results must be backed by clear formal claims or extensive controlled analyses to be convincing.

reviewer_b.name: Applied NLP Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in computational social science and applied NLP, specializing in social media analysis, weak supervision, and resource-scarce annotation settings.
traits: Rewards papers that propose practical, label-efficient methods (weak supervision / distant supervision) that improve real-world tasks even without formal proofs.
Gives positive credit for collection and planned release of large datasets and modeling artifacts that enable downstream research.
Favors methods that integrate social/behavioral signals and collective classification for improved performance on social media tasks; expects clear empirical demonstrations of benefit.
Prefers evaluations that show practical effect sizes and ablation over perfect theoretical guarantees (will accept smaller annotated subsets if approach is label-efficient).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritize practical impact, dataset contributions, and clear empirical evidence that a method helps real-world problems, even in the absence of deep theoretical analysis.
